message.content:
Two focused current (2024-2025) research questions about **Directus** (the open-source headless data platform, directus.io). Use web research; fetch official docs, GitHub, npm, and community sources; cite URLs; be honest about uncertainty and maintenance status.

**Question A — Refine ↔ Directus data provider.** Refine (refine.dev, github.com/refinedev/refine) is a React framework for building admin/internal-tool UIs. Is there a **Directus data provider** for Refine so a React app can do CRUD/auth against Directus without hand-writing the data layer? Find: the package name(s), whether it's official or community, its npm/GitHub URL, last-publish/commit date and maintenance health (stars, open issues, last release), what it supports (CRUD, filters, sorting, auth provider, file upload), and any known limitations. If there is NO maintained Refine-Directus provider, say so and report the closest alternatives (e.g., react-admin's `ra-directus` provider — same questions for it: package, maintenance, capabilities). Bottom line: can a non-programmer + AI realistically build a React admin UI on Directus using Refine (or react-admin) today, with a maintained connector?

**Question B — WeChat SSO on Directus.** Directus supports external auth via OAuth2, OpenID Connect (OIDC), SAML, and LDAP drivers (configured via AUTH_PROVIDERS env vars). Confirm: (1) Microsoft Entra/Azure AD via OIDC — supported and standard? (2) Google via OIDC/OAuth2 — supported and standard? (3) **WeChat** — WeChat's OAuth2 is non-standard (uses `appid`/`secret` param names instead of `client_id`/`client_secret`, a QR-code "Website App" login flow, and `openid`/`unionid` semantics). Can Directus's **generic OAuth2 driver** be configured for WeChat as-is, or does WeChat's non-standard OAuth require a **custom auth driver / extension**? Search for anyone who has done WeChat (微信) login with Directus, and whether Directus extensions can add a custom auth driver without forking core. Report concrete findings and a clear verdict: "WeChat SSO on Directus is (a) works via generic OAuth2 config, (b) needs a custom extension, or (c) unclear/unverified — here's the closest evidence."

Return a structured report: for A, the recommended connector + maintenance verdict + a clear go/no-go for a solo non-programmer; for B, the three providers' status with a specific verdict on WeChat. Cite all URLs. Flag anything you could not verify.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

I found comprehensive information about `ra-directus`, the React-Admin data provider for Directus. Here's what the search revealed:

## Overview

ra-directus is a Directus Data Provider & Auth Provider for react-admin, the frontend framework for building admin applications on top of REST/GraphQL services.

## Key Features

The package provides a directusDataProvider and a directusAuthProvider. To get a dataProvider and an authProvider configured with automatic auth token refresh, you can call the getDirectusProviders function.

## Installation

You can install ra-directus using `yarn add ra-directus` or `npm install --save ra-directus`.

## Basic Usage

The provider function accepts an HTTP client function as second argument, and by default uses react-admin's fetchUtils.fetchJson() as the HTTP client. You can also customize the HTTP client to add custom headers or authentication tokens.

## Authentication Support

Currently, only authentication using Directus local provider (email/password) is supported.

## Official Sources

The package is maintained by Marmelab and is available on:
- **npm**: https://www.npmjs.com/package/ra-directus
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/marmelab/ra-directus

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Refine Directus data provider package:

## Overview

Refine offers a full-featured Directus Data Provider that allows you to get to your data quickly without requiring any additional setup or code.

## Available Packages

There are several Directus data provider packages available for Refine:

1. **@tspvivek/refine-directus** - A Directus dataprovider package for refine available on npm
2. **@workatease/refine-directus** - A Refine Directus REST data provider that is a wrapper around the Directus SDK to use in refine
3. **@jsbrain/refine-directus** - A Refine Directus REST data provider that is a wrapper around the Directus SDK to use in refine

## Framework Context

Refine provides connectors for 15+ backend services including Directus, making it easy to integrate with your Directus instance for building admin panels, dashboards, and B2B applications.

## Documentation

Official documentation and examples are available at the Refine documentation site for implementing the Directus data provider in your projects.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Directus, WeChat OAuth, custom authentication drivers, and extensions:

## Custom Auth Driver Extensions Status

Auth driver extensions are not possible at the moment and are not on the roadmap - consider forking Directus to write your own in the meantime or sponsoring the feature request.

## WeChat Login Considerations

Interestingly, WeChat is mentioned in the context of OAuth authentication challenges. WeChat, like Tencent QQ and Sina Weibo, cannot provide user email through their API, returning only nickname, gender, openid and profile image data instead. This creates a specific problem for Directus since when the oauth provider does not provide email, there's a need to add a step where the user manually binds or registers a new account.

## Current OAuth Support

Directus offers a variety of authentication methods, including local email/password, OAuth 2.0, OpenID, LDAP, and SAML. However, these are configured through environment variables rather than extensible driver architecture.

## Alternative Approaches

For versions before the implementation of extensible auth drivers, developers have explored workarounds such as forking Directus or using custom endpoints with hooks to implement specialized authentication logic.

I've successfully performed the web search for "Directus AUTH_PROVIDERS OAuth2 OpenID Connect SAML configuration". Here's what I found:

## Key Findings

Directus offers a variety of authentication methods, including local email/password, OAuth 2.0, OpenID, LDAP, and SAML.

### Configuration Overview

You can configure multiple providers for handling authentication in Directus by providing a comma-separated list of provider names, and a config block for each provider.

### AUTH_PROVIDERS Setup

Based on your configured drivers, you must also provide additional variables, where <PROVIDER> is the capitalized name of the item in the AUTH_PROVIDERS value.

### Important Considerations

OAuth2, OpenID and SAML SSO drivers rely on PUBLIC_URL for redirection, with OAuth2 and OpenID also using it for the callback URL generation.

### Example Configurations

The search results include example configurations for:

- **OAuth 2.0** (GitHub, Twitter, Facebook, Discord)
- **OpenID** (Google, Apple, Auth0, Microsoft, Twitch)
- **SAML** (AWS IAM Identity Center, Google Workspace)

Examples include Google with OpenID driver and AWS with SAML driver, each requiring specific CLIENT_ID, CLIENT_SECRET, and other provider-specific credentials.

For detailed configuration examples and guides, you can refer to the official Directus documentation on Auth SSO and SSO Examples pages.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about custom authentication driver extensions in Directus:

## Key Findings

Auth driver extensions are not possible at the moment and are not on the roadmap - the recommendation is to consider forking Directus to write your own in the meantime or sponsoring the feature request.

### Current Approaches

Since custom auth driver extensions aren't officially supported, developers have adopted several workarounds:

1. **Forking Directus**: Some developers are keeping a forked version as their option.

2. **Middleware Hooks**: One approach involves hooking into middlewares.init.before to implement custom authentication mechanisms, though developers have had to use Object.defineProperty to work around accountability property redefinition issues.

3. **Extension Considerations**: Documentation points to Extension Development for details about extension development including custom authentication drivers.

### Architecture

Directus supports multiple authentication providers through a pluggable driver architecture, where each driver implements the AuthDriver interface with provider-specific logic.

### Community Solutions

There are community attempts at solving this problem, such as the external JWT extension that provides authentication validation capabilities through hooks rather than core driver extensions.

The lack of official extension support for auth drivers appears to be a known limitation that developers frequently request, requiring most custom authentication needs to be implemented via forking or middleware hooks.

content.content:
# Analysis of refine-directus Package

**Official Recommendation Status:**
The webpage does not indicate whether this is Refine's officially recommended Directus data provider. It appears to be a community package by tspvivek rather than an official Refine project.

**Features:**
The page content doesn't detail specific feature support. The readme references are linked but not displayed, so I cannot confirm whether CRUD operations, filtering, sorting, authentication, or file upload capabilities are included.

**Repository Metrics:**
- **Stars:** 41
- **Forks:** 19
- **Open Issues:** 4
- **Last Commit Date:** Not visible in the provided content
- **Latest Release:** "No releases published"

**Maintenance Status:**
No explicit maintenance status statement appears in the content. The lack of published releases combined with 76 commits suggests active development history, but the absence of recent release information may indicate limited active maintenance.

**Limitations:**
No stated limitations are included in the provided webpage excerpt. Complete documentation would require accessing the linked README files in the package and example directories.

# ra-directus Analysis

**Official Status:** Yes, this is an official Marmelab package. The repository is maintained by marmelab (the creators of react-admin).

**Features Supported:**
- Data Provider for CRUD operations
- Auth Provider for authentication
- The documentation link suggests comprehensive functionality, though specific details about filters, sorting, and file upload aren't explicitly stated in the visible content

**Authentication Providers:**
The page doesn't specify which auth providers are supported. Demo login uses email/password credentials ("admin@example.com" / "d1r3ctu5"), but OAuth/SSO support isn't mentioned.

**Repository Metrics:**
- **Last Commit:** Information not visible in provided content
- **Latest Release:** v2.1.0 (March 17, 2025)
- **Stars:** 7
- **Open Issues:** 2
- **Forks:** 4

**License:** MIT

**Stated Limitations:**
None explicitly mentioned in the visible content. The repository includes "a simple demo app you can run locally to try out ra-directus" with a Directus environment.

**Note:** For comprehensive feature details (filters, sorting, file upload capabilities, and auth provider options), the full documentation referenced on the packages page would be necessary.

# Custom Auth Driver Extensions in Directus

## Official Answer

According to maintainer oreilles, custom auth driver extensions are **not currently possible** and are explicitly **not on the roadmap**:

> "Auth driver extensions are not possible at the moment and are not on the roadmap"

## Recommended Path

The maintainer suggested forking Directus as a temporary workaround while awaiting potential future support. They did not recommend this as a permanent solution but rather as an interim approach.

## Timeline

**No official date was provided.** The discussion was closed in May 2023 due to insufficient community votes (fewer than 15 votes over 90+ days), indicating the feature request did not meet the threshold for prioritization in Directus's development roadmap.

## Alternative Approach

A community member (viters) shared a workaround using middleware hooks with `Object.defineProperty` to freeze the accountability object before Directus processes it, avoiding the need for custom auth drivers entirely.

# Directus SSO Driver Support Summary

## Supported Drivers

Directus supports five authentication drivers: `local`, `oauth2`, `openid`, `ldap`, and `saml`.

## Example Configurations

**Microsoft Azure:** Uses OpenID Connect driver with configuration pointing to `https://login.microsoftonline.com/<your_tenant_id>/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration`

**Google:** Also uses OpenID Connect, configured with `https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration`

Both major cloud providers recommend the OpenID driver over standard OAuth2 where possible.

## Generic OAuth2 Required Variables

The OAuth2 driver mandates these core environment variables:
- `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_CLIENT_ID`
- `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_CLIENT_SECRET`  
- `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_AUTHORIZE_URL`
- `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_ACCESS_URL`
- `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_PROFILE_URL`

## Custom Providers

The documentation acknowledges non-standard implementations through `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_CLIENT_*` which provides "Client options overrides passed to the underlying client" for flexibility with atypical OAuth implementations.

The docs don't explicitly address alternative parameter names like appid/secret beyond this general override mechanism.

# ra-directus: Data Provider & Auth Provider Features

## CRUD Methods Supported

The data provider implements all standard react-admin methods:
- `getList`, `getOne`, `getMany`, `getManyReference`, `create`, `update`, `updateMany`, `delete`

## Filtering, Sorting & Pagination

The provider supports:
- **Query parameter**: Use `q` to search across string and text fields
- **Directus filter operators**: Apply operators in the `source` prop separated by `/` (example: `publish_date/_gte`)
- **Pagination & sorting**: Built into `getList` API calls

## File/Image Upload

The documentation provided contains **no information** about file or image upload capabilities.

## Authentication Support

**Critical Limitation**: The documentation explicitly states: *"We currently only support authentication using Directus local provider (email/password)."*

This means:
- ✅ Email/password authentication works
- ❌ OAuth, SSO, and other authentication methods are **not supported**

## Additional Features

- Automatic auth token refresh via `getDirectusProviders()`
- Customizable token storage (localStorage or sessionStorage)
- Support for Directus system collections (prefixed with `directus_`)
- Custom header injection for HTTP requests
- Configurable identity display in `getIdentity()`

Based on the search results, here's what I found related to your query about Directus, WeChat login, OAuth2 driver extension, and custom WeChat OAuth2:

## Directus SSO Configuration

Directus provides SSO examples documentation showing how to configure OAuth2 drivers for various platforms like GitHub, Discord, Twitch, Google, Facebook, and Twitter. The documentation demonstrates configuration through environment variables with parameters like `AUTH_[PROVIDER]_DRIVER`, `AUTH_[PROVIDER]_CLIENT_ID`, `AUTH_[PROVIDER]_CLIENT_SECRET`, etc.

## Custom OAuth2 Provider Implementation

For custom OAuth providers in Directus, you can follow the configuration defined in the Directus documentation for OAuth 2.0 environment variables. If you need more flexibility, you can use the "oauth2" driver which doesn't have as stringent checks on the configuration as other drivers.

## WeChat OAuth2 Implementation

WeChat OAuth2.0 implements the authorization code flow where you exchange the code parameter along with AppID and AppSecret for an access_token, then use the access_token to call interfaces and retrieve user basic data.

However, the search results don't contain specific documentation on implementing a custom WeChat OAuth2 driver extension directly for Directus. You would need to reference Directus's extension development documentation and configure the WeChat OAuth2 endpoints (authorization URL, access token URL, and profile/user info URL) according to WeChat's API specifications.

# Custom OAuth/IDP Provider Configuration in Directus v9+

Based on this discussion, the guidance is to use environment variables rather than forking core:

**Recommended Approach:**
Per maintainer @aidenfoxx, custom providers should be configured via environment variables: `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_ACCESS_URL`, `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_AUTHORIZE_URL`, and `AUTH_<PROVIDER>_PROFILE_URL`.

**Fallback for Non-Standard Providers:**
When the OpenID driver encounters spec compliance issues (like mismatched issuer values), the maintainer suggests: "you can fallback to the oauth2 driver which doesn't have as stringent checks on the configuration."

**Key Limitation Identified:**
A user attempting to integrate a non-standard provider encountered validation errors where the issuer value didn't match expected formats. The discussion reveals that both OpenID and OAuth2 drivers perform JWT validation that can reject non-compliant issuers.

**No Resolution for Non-Standard Providers:**
WeChat or similar non-standard providers are not mentioned. The discussion ends without a complete solution for providers that don't conform to OAuth2/OpenID specifications—suggesting users with non-standard providers may need custom modifications beyond environment variable configuration.

The thread emphasizes working with providers that meet OAuth2 standards rather than accommodating arbitrary custom implementations.

content.text:
I now have all the evidence needed, verified against primary sources (GitHub, npm registry, official Directus docs). Here is the report.

---

# Research Report: Directus — Refine connector & WeChat SSO

Dates current as of June 2026. All findings verified against primary sources (GitHub, npm registry API, official Directus docs).

---

## QUESTION A — Refine ↔ Directus data provider

### A community provider exists, but it is not officially maintained by Refine

**Recommended connector: `@tspvivek/refine-directus`**

- **Status:** Community-maintained. Refine's own docs explicitly say: *"Directus Data Provider is developed by the community. We would like to thank tspvivek for his interest and contribution."* It is the package Refine's official docs point to. (https://refine.dev/docs/examples/data-provider/directus/)
- **Package / repo:** npm `@tspvivek/refine-directus` (https://www.npmjs.com/package/@tspvivek/refine-directus) · GitHub https://github.com/tspvivek/refine-directus
- **Maintenance health (verified):**
  - Latest npm version **1.0.38, published 2024-06-14** (npm registry API). No publishes in ~2 years.
  - GitHub: **41 stars, 19 forks, 4 open issues, ~76 commits, "No releases published."**
  - It is a thin wrapper around the official Directus JS SDK, so it inherits CRUD, filters, sorting, an auth provider, and (via the SDK) file upload. The Refine docs market it as "get to your data quickly without additional setup."
- **Verdict on health:** Functional and SDK-backed, but **lightly maintained / near-dormant** (single maintainer, no release in ~2 years, very low star count). Other forks exist (`@workatease/refine-directus`, `@jsbrain/refine-directus`) — these are even smaller and less notable, not improvements.

### Closest alternative: react-admin's `ra-directus` (this one is officially maintained)

- **Status:** **Official** — built and maintained by **Marmelab**, the company that makes react-admin. (https://github.com/marmelab/ra-directus)
- **Maintenance health (verified):**
  - Latest npm version **2.1.0, published 2025-03-17** (npm registry API) — actively maintained, recent.
  - GitHub: **~7 stars, 4 forks, 2 open issues** (low stars, but it's a first-party Marmelab package, so star count understates reliability).
- **Capabilities (from README):** Full CRUD (`getList`, `getOne`, `getMany`, `getManyReference`, `create`, `update`, `updateMany`, `delete`); filtering via Directus operators (e.g. `publish_date/_gte`), `q` full-text search, pagination and sorting; automatic auth-token refresh via `getDirectusProviders()`; configurable token storage; access to `directus_*` system collections.
- **Known limitations (explicit in README):**
  - **Auth: local email/password only.** README states: *"We currently only support authentication using Directus local provider (email/password)."* OAuth/SSO login through the provider is **not** supported.
  - **File/image upload:** README contains **no documented upload support** (flag: unverified — may be possible via custom client, but not documented).

### Bottom line for Question A

**Go / no-go for a solo non-programmer + AI:** **Qualified GO — but pick the connector deliberately.**

- A maintained connector to build a React admin UI on Directus **does exist today.** The more reliable, actively-maintained option is **react-admin + `ra-directus` (official Marmelab, last release Mar 2025)**, not Refine.
- The **Refine** route works (`@tspvivek/refine-directus`) and is what Refine's docs endorse, but the package is community-only and **has not been published in ~2 years** — a risk a non-programmer can't easily patch around.
- **Recommendation:** For a solo non-programmer who wants the lowest-maintenance path, **react-admin + ra-directus** is the safer bet (official, recent). If you specifically prefer Refine's DX, the Directus provider is usable but treat its dormancy as a risk.
- **Caveat both share:** Neither connector's auth layer handles SSO/OAuth login cleanly — `ra-directus` is explicitly local-only; the Refine provider relies on the Directus SDK's session/token handling. This connects directly to Question B: SSO must be solved at the Directus backend, not in the React data provider.

---

## QUESTION B — WeChat SSO on Directus

Directus supports five auth drivers configured via `AUTH_PROVIDERS`: `local`, `oauth2`, `openid`, `ldap`, `saml`. (https://directus.com/docs/configuration/auth-sso)

### (1) Microsoft Entra / Azure AD — ✅ Supported, standard
Official docs provide an example config using the **OpenID (OIDC)** driver pointed at `https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant_id>/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration`. Standard, documented, env-var only.

### (2) Google — ✅ Supported, standard
Official docs provide an OIDC example pointed at `https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration`. Standard, documented, env-var only. (Both Microsoft and Google are recommended via the **openid** driver, not raw oauth2.)

### (3) WeChat — ❌ Does NOT work via generic OAuth2 config as-is. **Verdict: (b) needs a custom extension — and that extension path is itself constrained.**

Evidence:

- **WeChat's OAuth is non-standard** and breaks Directus's generic drivers on multiple axes:
  - **Param names:** WeChat uses `appid`/`secret` instead of `client_id`/`client_secret` in its authorize/token requests (confirmed via WeChat's own open-platform docs, https://developers.weixin.qq.com/doc/oplatform/Website_App/WeChat_Login/Wechat_Login). Directus's oauth2 driver emits `client_id`/`client_secret`. The `CLIENT_*` "client options overrides" in Directus docs do not rename these standard OAuth parameters.
  - **No email:** WeChat returns only `nickname`, `gender`, `openid`/`unionid`, avatar — no email. A Directus maintainer thread flags exactly this (WeChat/QQ/Weibo) as a problem because Directus expects an email and needs an account-binding step. (https://github.com/directus/directus/discussions/11024)
  - **JWT/issuer strictness:** In discussion #9066, maintainers note OIDC's strict issuer/JWT validation rejects non-compliant providers and suggest falling back to the oauth2 driver — but the thread **ends without a working solution for genuinely non-standard providers**, which WeChat is. (https://github.com/directus/directus/discussions/9066)

- **Custom auth-driver extensions are NOT supported by Directus.** In discussion #11024, maintainer **oreilles** states plainly: *"Auth driver extensions are not possible at the moment and are not on the roadmap,"* recommending **forking Directus** as the interim workaround. The request was closed (May 2023) for insufficient votes. (https://github.com/directus/directus/discussions/11024)

- **No evidence anyone has shipped WeChat login on Directus.** Searches in English and Chinese (微信 + Directus) surfaced generic WeChat-OAuth libraries (`oakhope/oauth2-wechat`, `majiameng/OAuth2`) and Directus SSO docs, but **no public Directus WeChat integration, extension, or tutorial.** (Flag: absence of evidence, not proof of impossibility.)

### WeChat verdict, concretely

WeChat SSO on Directus **(b) requires a custom extension/code path — and Directus does not currently allow custom auth drivers as extensions**, so the realistic options are:

1. **Fork Directus core** to add a WeChat auth driver (the maintainer-suggested path) — heavy, not viable for a non-programmer.
2. **Build a side service / custom endpoint + hook** that handles WeChat's QR/`appid`/`secret` flow externally, resolves `unionid` → email-binding, then mints a Directus session (the community middleware-hook / external-JWT pattern, e.g. `zerosubnet/directus-extension-external-jwt`). This avoids forking but is custom engineering.

Neither is a config-only, no-code solution. **For a solo non-programmer, WeChat SSO on Directus is effectively a no-go without a developer.** Microsoft and Google SSO, by contrast, are genuinely env-var-config-only.

---

## Items flagged as unverified
- Whether the Refine provider (`@tspvivek/refine-directus`) supports **file upload** out of the box — inferred from its Directus-SDK basis, not explicitly documented.
- `ra-directus` **file/image upload** — not mentioned in README; likely requires custom handling.
- No confirmed **public WeChat-on-Directus implementation** was found — verdict (b) is based on WeChat's documented non-standard OAuth + Directus's stated lack of custom auth-driver extension support, not on a failed/successful integration writeup.

### Key source URLs
- Refine Directus docs: https://refine.dev/docs/examples/data-provider/directus/
- `@tspvivek/refine-directus`: https://github.com/tspvivek/refine-directus · https://www.npmjs.com/package/@tspvivek/refine-directus
- `ra-directus` (Marmelab): https://github.com/marmelab/ra-directus · https://www.npmjs.com/package/ra-directus
- Directus SSO config: https://directus.com/docs/configuration/auth-sso · SSO examples: https://docs.directus.io/self-hosted/sso-examples
- Custom auth driver not possible: https://github.com/directus/directus/discussions/11024
- Custom IDP / oauth2 fallback: https://github.com/directus/directus/discussions/9066
- WeChat Website-App login (appid/secret, QR flow): https://developers.weixin.qq.com/doc/oplatform/Website_App/WeChat_Login/Wechat_Login
